turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Breyer has our opinion this morning in case 15-1503, Turner versus United States and the consolidated case.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Well, this is a case in which the seven petitioners were convicted of murdering a woman, Catherine Fuller, in 1984. Many years later, they found that the Government had withheld certain evidence and they argued their due process rights were violated because the withheld evidence was, one, favorable to the defense, and also material. So they asked for a new trial, under Brady versus Maryland, a well-known case. And the Superior Court in the District of Columbia held an evidentiary hearing, reviewed the evidence, and it said the evidence was not material. The Government should not have withheld it, but it was not material, it did not make any difference, and so it would not grant them a new trial. And the D.C. Court of Appeals reviewed it and agreed with that. We are doing the same. We are reviewing the record, which we have done, and now we agree with the lower courts, we think the evidence was not material. That is, it would not have made a difference if the Government had disclosed it. The government's theory at trial was that Fuller was killed by a large group of people that included the petitioners in notice. Two of them, Calvin Alston and Harry Bennett, confessed to being part of that sizable group. And they testified in some detail about the whole attack, which is pretty gruesome. Melvin Montgomery was a witness. He testified he was in the park with them when they pointed to the target person and said they were going to rob her. And there was another witness, Maurice Thomas, who said he was walking home when he saw the group, including petitioners, beating her up in an alley. And then there were two others who said that they saw petitioners and others beating Fuller. And there was other evidence as well. It gives you a flavor for it, you would have to look into the record. The undisclosed evidence consisted of, really, the following. First, there was the identity of a man that the petitioners and their lawyers knew had been seen running through the alley but they did not have his identity, and the Government had not disclosed that. He had run through the alley after the body was discovered. That person, whose name was not disclosed had robbed two women in the months before the trial. And second, the Government withheld some notes from an interview with another person named Willie Luchie. He had said that he and three others had walked through the alley 30 minutes before the body was discovered and they did not see anybody, but they did hear groans coming from the garage. And then the Government withheld notes from an interview with a person named Amy Davis. And she implicated yet another person called James Blue, but the problem was she had implicated a lot of people in a lot of murders before, at least some, and they had not turned out to be very good leads. And finally, there was some other impeachment evidence. Now, the only question before us, as I said, was materiality. The petitioners have to show there is, to be more specific about it, a reasonable probability that, had the evidence been disclosed, the result of the trial would have been different. Now, what we have done is we have read the record, we have looked through it, it is summarized in the opinion, and the conclusion is, just like the lower courts, we think they did not meet that standard. They say, “If we had only had this, we would have had a totally different kind of defense. We would have tried to say it was just that one man,” but it is just, in our view, too much evidence against them. What they point to is too little, too weak, or too distant to undermine the many evidences -- the many people -- the many witnesses that they had at the trial. And the impeachment evidence was largely accumulative of evidence that was already presented. So, we have gone through the record. We summarized it. You can read it. There is a dissent, but the majority of us, we affirm the judgment of the lower court. Justice Kagan has written a dissent which Justice Ginsburg has joined. And Justice Gorsuch took no part in the consideration or decision of the case.
